content.body:
**Sound** is a **pressure wave** — a vibrating source compresses and expands air molecules, creating alternating regions of high and low pressure that travel outward.

Key properties:
- **1-dimensional signal:** amplitude varies over time (unlike images, which are 2D)
- **Continuous (analog):** the wave is a smooth, unbroken curve in reality

**Wave behaviors:**
- **Reflection:** the wave bounces off surfaces
- **Refraction:** the wave changes angle when entering a different medium
- **Diffraction:** the wave bends around obstacles

- **Frequency:** an **objective, physical** measurement — cycles per second (Hz). A440 has exactly 440 oscillation cycles per second.
- **Pitch:** a **subjective, perceptual** quality — how 'high' or 'low' a sound seems to a listener.

Frequency and pitch are closely related but not identical. Two sounds at the same frequency may be perceived at different pitches depending on context.

**Octave = doubling of frequency.** A4 = 440 Hz, next A up = 880 Hz, next = 1760 Hz.

Real instruments do not produce a single pure frequency — they produce a **fundamental frequency** plus **integer multiples** called harmonics.

Example (A4 = 440 Hz): 440, 880, 1320, 1760 Hz…

The mix of harmonics gives each instrument its unique **timbre** (sound color) — why a violin and a piano playing A4 sound different even at the same pitch.

**Digitization** converts a continuous analog signal into discrete numbers. It has **two independent dimensions**:

1. **Sampling (in time):** measure the amplitude at evenly spaced time intervals. The rate = **sampling frequency** (Hz)
2. **Quantization (in amplitude):** round each measured value to the nearest discrete level. With **N bits**, there are $2^N$ possible levels

**Three questions to answer when digitizing audio:**
1. What sampling rate?
2. How many bits (quantization depth)?
3. What file format?

**CD Audio standard:** 44,100 Hz, 16 bits per sample, 2 channels (stereo)
Sampling ≠ Quantization. Sampling happens in the **time domain** (how often you measure). Quantization happens in the **amplitude domain** (how precisely you measure). They are two separate steps.

**The Aliasing Problem**

If you sample too slowly, the samples no longer represent the original signal correctly — they appear to come from a completely different, lower frequency. This wrong detected frequency is called an **alias**.

rows: Sample taken at same point every cycle
All samples identical → detected as 0 Hz (DC)
Alias: f_alias = 1.5f − f = 0.5f (half the real frequency)

content.body:
To correctly capture (and later reconstruct) a signal, the **sampling rate must be at least twice the maximum frequency** present in the signal:

$$f_{sampling} \geq 2 \times f_{max}$$

- **Nyquist rate** = $2 \times f_{max}$ — the minimum acceptable sampling rate
- **Nyquist frequency** = $\frac{f_{sampling}}{2}$ — the highest frequency that can be correctly captured at a given sampling rate

content.label: Alias Frequency Formula (Eq. 6.1)
content.expression: f_{alias} = f_{sampling} - f_{true} \quad \text{(when } f_{true} < f_{sampling} < 2 \times f_{true}\text{)}

content.title: Example 1 — No Aliasing
content.body:
**Sound up to 4 kHz, sampled at 10 kHz.**

Nyquist rate = 2 × 4,000 = **8,000 Hz**

10,000 ≥ 8,000 → sampling rate is above the Nyquist rate → **no aliasing**. The signal is correctly captured.

content.title: Example 2 — Aliasing
content.body:
**5 kHz sound, sampled at 8 kHz.**

Nyquist rate = 2 × 5,000 = 10,000 Hz

8,000 < 10,000 → sampling rate is too low → **aliasing occurs**.

$$f_{alias} = 8{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 3{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$

You hear a 3 kHz tone instead of the real 5 kHz tone.

content.title: Example 3 — Why CD Uses 44.1 kHz
content.body:
**Human hearing goes up to ~20 kHz.**

Nyquist rate = 2 × 20,000 = 40,000 Hz (minimum)

CD uses **44,100 Hz** — above the minimum, giving a safety margin and room for the anti-aliasing filter to roll off smoothly.

content.title: Example 4 — Telephone Bandwidth
content.body:
**Telephone bandwidth: 200–3,400 Hz.**

Maximum frequency = 3,400 Hz → Nyquist rate = 2 × 3,400 = **6,800 Hz**

In practice, telephone systems use **8,000 Hz** — a comfortable margin above the Nyquist rate.

A **low-pass filter** applied to the analog signal **before sampling**. It removes (attenuates) all frequency components above the Nyquist frequency ($f_{sampling}/2$), preventing them from folding back as aliases.

Without this filter, any high-frequency content above the Nyquist frequency would alias down into the audible band and corrupt the recording.

The Nyquist Theorem is the most important concept in this section. Know the formula $f_{sampling} \geq 2 \times f_{max}$, know the alias formula $f_{alias} = f_{sampling} - f_{true}$, and be able to check whether aliasing occurs and calculate the alias frequency.

flashcards.q: What is sound, physically? How is it different from an image signal?
flashcards.a: Sound is a **pressure wave** — air molecules compressed and expanded by a vibrating source. It is a **1-dimensional signal** (amplitude varies over time only). Images are 2D (amplitude varies over x and y position).

flashcards.q: What is the difference between frequency and pitch?
flashcards.a: **Frequency** is objective — it measures cycles per second (Hz). **Pitch** is subjective — it is how high or low a sound *feels* to a listener. A sound at 440 Hz always has that frequency, but pitch perception can vary by context.

flashcards.q: What are harmonics, and why do they matter?
flashcards.a: Harmonics are **integer multiples of a fundamental frequency**. Example: 440 Hz → 880, 1320, 1760 Hz. Real instruments produce a mixture of harmonics — the specific mix gives each instrument its **timbre** (sound color). This is why different instruments sound different on the same note.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between sampling and quantization?
flashcards.a: **Sampling** works in the **time domain** — it decides *how often* to measure. **Quantization** works in the **amplitude domain** — it decides *how precisely* to measure each value by rounding to the nearest of $2^N$ levels.

flashcards.q: What does the Nyquist Theorem say, exactly?
flashcards.a: The **sampling rate must be at least twice the maximum frequency** in the signal: $f_{sampling} \geq 2 \times f_{max}$. Below this rate, aliasing occurs — you measure a wrong (lower) frequency instead of the real one.

flashcards.q: What is aliasing? What causes it?
flashcards.a: Aliasing is when a signal is sampled too slowly and the samples appear to represent a **different, lower frequency** — an 'alias' of the real signal. Cause: $f_{sampling} < 2 \times f_{true}$. The alias frequency is $f_{alias} = f_{sampling} - f_{true}$.

flashcards.q: What is the Nyquist frequency, and how is it different from the Nyquist rate?
flashcards.a:
- **Nyquist rate** = $2 \times f_{max}$ — the *minimum sampling rate* you need to avoid aliasing
- **Nyquist frequency** = $f_{sampling}/2$ — the *highest frequency* you can correctly capture at a given sampling rate

flashcards.q: Why does CD audio use 44,100 Hz if human hearing only goes to 20 kHz?
flashcards.a: Nyquist rate = 2 × 20,000 = 40,000 Hz. CD uses 44,100 Hz — **above the minimum** — for two reasons: (1) a safety margin in case some content approaches 20 kHz, and (2) to give the anti-aliasing filter room to roll off smoothly without cutting audible frequencies.

problems.q: A sound has a maximum frequency of 6 kHz and is sampled at 9 kHz. (a) Does aliasing occur? (b) If yes, what is the alias frequency?
problems.a:
(a) Nyquist rate = 2 × 6,000 = **12,000 Hz**. Sampling rate 9,000 < 12,000 → **yes, aliasing occurs**.

(b) $f_{alias} = f_{sampling} - f_{true} = 9{,}000 - 6{,}000 = \mathbf{3{,}000 \text{ Hz}}$

You hear a 3 kHz tone instead of the real 6 kHz tone.
problems.hint: Check: is f_sampling ≥ 2 × f_true? If not, use f_alias = f_sampling − f_true

problems.q: A medical audio system must capture signals up to 8 kHz. What is the minimum sampling rate required?
problems.a:
Nyquist rate = $2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 8{,}000 = \mathbf{16{,}000 \text{ Hz}}$

The system must sample at **at least 16 kHz** to avoid aliasing.

problems.q: A system samples at 12 kHz. What is its Nyquist frequency — i.e., the highest frequency it can correctly capture?
problems.a:
Nyquist frequency = $\frac{f_{sampling}}{2} = \frac{12{,}000}{2} = \mathbf{6{,}000 \text{ Hz}}$

Any frequency above 6 kHz would alias. The anti-aliasing filter must block everything above 6 kHz.

problems.q: A 3 kHz tone is sampled at 3 kHz (sampling rate equals the tone frequency). What does the sampled signal look like, and what frequency does it represent?
problems.a:
When sampling rate equals the tone frequency, the sampler hits **the same phase point every cycle** — all samples are identical (e.g., all zeros if sampling at the zero crossing). The samples look like a **constant DC signal** — zero frequency.

This is a severe aliasing case: the measured frequency is 0 Hz instead of 3 kHz. The Nyquist theorem requires sampling at **≥ 2 × 3 = 6 kHz** to correctly capture this signal.
problems.hint: What does 'same point every cycle' mean for the measured value?
